exploration_log.aha_moment: The existing `realIP` function blindly takes the first (leftmost) IP from X-Forwarded-For. The fix is a right-to-left walk skipping trusted CIDRs — this is a standard pattern for reverse-proxy IP extraction.
exploration_log.hindsight: Could have added the CIDR list to the existing `RealIP` function via an options struct, but a new constructor is cleaner and backward-compatible.

alternatives: Modify RealIP to accept optional CIDRs via variadic args
key_decisions.decision: Add RealIPWithTrustedProxies as a separate constructor
key_decisions.reasoning: Keeps the existing RealIP middleware signature unchanged (backward compatibility) and cleanly separates the two behaviors.

search_sequence.reasoning: Task description pointed directly to this file.
search_sequence.result: The realIP helper function takes the FIRST (leftmost) IP from X-Forwarded-For without any proxy validation.

search_sequence.result: Tests cover X-Real-IP, X-Forwarded-For, header precedence, and invalid IP — none test trusted-proxy logic.

solve_notes: Added `RealIPWithTrustedProxies(trustedCIDRs []string)` as a new constructor alongside the existing `RealIP` to preserve backward compatibility. The new constructor parses CIDRs at initialization time and uses a new `realIPFromTrusted` helper that walks the X-Forwarded-For header right-to-left, skipping IPs in trusted networks. A small `isTrusted` helper checks CIDR membership.
task_text:
Fix RealIP middleware not validating X-Forwarded-For against trusted proxies

The `RealIP` middleware in `middleware/realip.go` blindly trusts the
first IP in the `X-Forwarded-For` header chain. An untrusted client
can spoof its IP by setting the header directly. Fix `RealIP` to accept
a configurable list of trusted proxy CIDRs and walk the
`X-Forwarded-For` chain from right to left, selecting the first IP
not in a trusted range.

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set contains only the three new definitions added. An AI agent would also read the existing `RealIP` and `realIP` functions to understand the established pattern (how middleware constructors are structured, how headers are accessed) before writing the new ones — skipping these would cause the agent to guess at the coding style and potentially backtrack.
